coredata.dc:title: In situ preparation of visible-light-driven carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 hybrid materials for the photoreduction of Cr(VI)

coredata.dc:description: In this study, different carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/NaBiO3 hybrid materials were synthesized as photocatalysts to effectively utilize visible light for the photocatalytic degradation of contaminants effectively. These hybrid materials exhibit an enhanced photocatalytic reduction of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in the aqueous medium. Zero-dimensional nanoparticles of CQDs were embedded within the two-dimensional NaBiO3 nanosheets by the hydrothermal process. Compared with that of the pure NaBiO3 nanosheets, the photocatalytic performance of the hybrid catalysts was significantly high and 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 catalyst exhibited better photocatalytic performance. We performed the first-principles density functional theory calculations to study the interfacial properties of pure NaBiO3 nanosheets and hybrid photocatalysts, and confirmed the CQDs played an important role in the CQDs/NaBiO3 composites. The experimental results indicated that the enhanced reduction of Cr(VI) was probably due to the high loading of CQDs (electron acceptor) on NaBiO3, which made NaBiO3 nanomaterials to respond in visible light and significantly improved their electron-hole separation efficiency.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NaBiO3 nanosheets, and X carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 (CQDs/NBO) (X = 3, 6, 10, 15, and 30 wt.%) hybrid materials.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of (a) pure NaBiO3 nanosheets, (b-c) 6 wt.% carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 (CQDs/NaBiO3) hybrid materials, (d-e) NaBiO3 and 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials and (f) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials; d: lattice spacing.

(a) Energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of the 6 wt.% CQDs/NBO sample, (b) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of 6 wt.% CQDs/NBO survey, (c) O 1 s, (d) C 1 s, (e) Bi 4f, and (f) Na 1 s spectra, respectively.
Structural models of (a) NaBiO3 and (b) 6 wt.% carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 composites; a: x axis, b: y axis, c: z axis.
Total density states (DOS) of (a) NaBiO3 nanosheets and (b) 6 wt.% CQDs/NBO composites; Partial density states (PDOS) of (c) NaBiO3 nanosheets and (d) 6 wt.% CQDs/NBO composites.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of NaBiO3 (NBO) and 6 wt.% CQDs/NBO composites.

(a) Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) photoreduction curves under visible light irradiation over X CQDs/NaBiO3 (X = 3, 6, 10, 15 and 30 wt.%) hybrid materials; (b) Effect of initial catalyst concentrations on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) for 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3; (c) Effect of solution pH on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) for prepared samples; (d) Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) over different normal anion under visible-light irradiation. Reaction conditions: Cr(VI) concentration 50 mg/L, catalyst dosage 1 g/L, pH = 6.5, light intensity 300 W (xenon lamp, λ > 400 nm); C 0 (mg/L): the Cr(VI) concentrations before removal; Ct (mg/L): the Cr(VI) concentrations after removal for t (min).

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of radical adducts trapped by spin-trap reagent (DMPO) (a) •O2 − and (b) •OH radicals in NaBiO3 and 6 wt.% carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 hybrid composites in the dark and under visible light irradiation. B: magnetic.
Photoreduction of Cr(VI) by 6 wt.% carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 composites with the addition of holes (h+), electrons (e−), •O2 − and •OH radicals scavengers. BQ: benzoquinone; TBA: isopropanol.
Schematic of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges in the carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 hybrid materials, and the possible reaction mechanism of Cr(VI) reduction.

head.ce:title: In situ preparation of visible-light-driven carbon quantum dots/NaBiO3 hybrid materials for the photoreduction of Cr(VI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, different carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/NaBiO3 hybrid materials were synthesized as photocatalysts to effectively utilize visible light for the photocatalytic degradation of contaminants effectively. These hybrid materials exhibit an enhanced photocatalytic reduction of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in the aqueous medium. Zero-dimensional nanoparticles of CQDs were embedded within the two-dimensional NaBiO3 nanosheets by the hydrothermal process. Compared with that of the pure NaBiO3 nanosheets, the photocatalytic performance of the hybrid catalysts was significantly high and 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 catalyst exhibited better photocatalytic performance. We performed the first-principles density functional theory calculations to study the interfacial properties of pure NaBiO3 nanosheets and hybrid photocatalysts, and confirmed the CQDs played an important role in the CQDs/NaBiO3 composites. The experimental results indicated that the enhanced reduction of Cr(VI) was probably due to the high loading of CQDs (electron acceptor) on NaBiO3, which made NaBiO3 nanomaterials to respond in visible light and significantly improved their electron-hole separation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid increase of electroplating, leather, chromate manufacturing and metallurgical industries have caused serious wastewater problems, and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is one of the common contaminant in industrial wastewater from industries, which possess high toxicity, high solubility and fluidity properties. The presence of Cr(VI) pose serious health and environment issues and can cause damages to aquatic and human life (Barrera-Díaz et al., 2012). To address this issue, various conventional methods, such as flotation, coagulation-flocculation, chemical precipitation, and ion exchange and adsorption, have been used to remove Cr(VI) ions (Barrera-Díaz et al., 2012). However, these reported methods have their shortcomings, including large amounts of sludge output, chemical expenditures, and excess processing costs, which limit their widespread applications. In chemical processing, photocatalysis is considered as a promising and environment friendly approach, which only uses solar luminescence energy. As trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) in aqueous solutions is much less toxic than Cr(VI), therefore, reducing Cr(VI) ions to Cr(III) by efficient photocatalytic reduction methods, have received extensive attention for the treatment of chromium-containing wastewater, because of its low energy consumption, simple operation and no secondary pollution (Marinho et al., 2017a, 2017b; Djellabi et al., 2016, 2017; Lei et al., 2017, 2016; Zhao et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019).

It has been reported that bismuth (Bi) has a narrow band gap and good visible light absorption capacity because of its unique outer electron structure (6s2) (Blasse et al., 1981; Lakshminarasimhan et al., 2008; Chang et al., 2010). Therefore, study on Bi-based oxides have received increased attention, and many trivalent Bi-containing photocatalysts, such as Bi2O3 (Ai et al., 2011; He et al., 2017; Lopes et al., 2017), Bi2O2CO3 (Hu et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2016), BiOCl (Huang et al., 2014), and Bi2MoO6 (Yu et al., 2019; Qiao et al., 2018), have also been developed. On the contrary, there are few studies on pentavalent Bi-based catalysts, such as NaBiO3, that exhibits a band gap of approximately 2.66 eV. NaBiO3 has a unique layered structure, and the conduction band (CB) is composed of mixed Na 3 s, Bi 6 s and O 2p orbitals. The strong dispersion of CB inhibits electron-hole recombination in NaBiO3 (Kako et al., 2007; Kou et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2013; Ding et al., 2015; Selvarajan et al., 2018). Its photocatalytic activity is very low due to poor light absorption so it has been considered as an unconventional but a useful visible light photocatalyst.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with sizes less than 10 nm. They exhibit outstanding optical properties, high chemical stability, low toxicity, and are easy and low-cost in production. In general, CQDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles, that mainly containing graphite carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphite oxide sheets (Di et al., 2015). Because of the conjugate structure of CQDs, they exhibit excellent electron transfer and storage performance. Moreover, the up-conversion performance of CQDs enhances light absorption (Huang et al., 2017a). All these properties allow CQDs to be promising candidates for modifying nanomaterials to improve their light absorption efficiency for enhanced photocatalytic activity. Some of the examples include CQDs/Bi2WO6 (Di et al., 2015), CQDs/ZnFe2O4 (Huang et al., 2017a), and CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction (Zhang et al., 2017).

Recently, observed that the CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials respond to visible light, and showed excellent Cr(VI) reduction efficiency when compared with previous photocatalytic materials (Appendix A Tables S1 and S2). In this study, we synthesized of X CQDs-NaBiO3 (X = 3, 6, 10, 15, and 30 wt.%) hybrid materials with varying percentage of CQDs, and observed that 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 showed optimal reduction efficiency (Appendix A Table S3). Further, the morphology, microstructures and optical properties of the prepared catalysts were investigated in detail. The role played by the composites in enhancing photocatalytic activity was elucidated, and a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. As far as authors knowledge, this is the first study using the combination of NaBiO3 and CQDs as photocatalyst for reduction of Cr(VI) contamination from water.

All the chemicals and reagents were used as received from commercial suppliers without any further purification. Sodium bismuthate dihydrate (NaBiO3⋅2H2O) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchase from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Company (China). L-asorbic acid was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagents Company. Ethylene glycol (AR, ≥ 99.0%) was supplied by Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Distilled water was used to carry out all experiments.
CQDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal process, as previously described (Wu et al., 2012).

The CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. In a typical hydrothermal process, commercial (NaBiO3⋅2H2O) was added to 60 mL of 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution and vigorously stirred for 2 hr. Further, a 20 mL aqueous solution of CQDs was added to the NaOH solution and stirred again for 2 hr. The above reaction mixture was placed inside a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 120°C for 12 hr and subsequently allowed to cool at room temperature. The precipitate obtained was dried at 120°C for 4 hr (Selvarajan et al., 2018).

The structure and crystallinity of the synthesized NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) diffractometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker AXS Co. Ltd, Germany), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM)(FEI TECNAI G20, FEI Co. Ltd, UK). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-1010, JEOL Japan Electronics Co., Ltd, Japan) was used to confirm the lattice spacing of the composites. The surface composition and chemical state of samples were further confirmed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (JEM-1010, JEOL Japan Electronics Co., Ltd, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (LESCALAB 250 Xi, VG Scientific, Germany). The specific surface area tests were performed with Quantachrome Instruments and analyzed by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) (Quantachrome Autosorb-1-C-MS apparatus, Quantachrome Instruments Co. Ltd, UK). The optical absorption characteristics of the hybrid materials were investigated using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) (Shimadzu UV-2550, Suzhou Arain Instrument Co., Ltd, China), and the Photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (FLS 980, Zhuohao Laboratory Equipment Co., Ltd. China) was used to further indicate the spread of the absorption range and photogenerated charge separation. The photocurrent was recorded using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Chinese Science Day Ltd, China) with a three-electrode cell system.

The photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was carried out in a circular quartz vessel with a water circulation system to maintain the temperature at approximately 25°C. A 300 W xenon lamp (PL-X300D, Beijing Princes Technology Co., Ltd, China) with a UV-cutoff filter (λ < 420 nm) was used as the light source. In a typical experiment, 30 to 60 mg of the synthesized catalyst was added to 30 mL of Cr(VI) solution (50 mg/L, based on Cr in K2Cr2O7 solution at room temperature). Initially, a certain amount of photocatalyst was added to the solution and stirred for 60 min under dark conditions to achieve adsorption equilibrium. After the light was turned on, 2 mL of the solution was sampled within a prescribed time. The impurities and photocatalyst from the collected sample were filtered off using a 0.22 μm filter, and the concentration of Cr(VI) was measured by the diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method on the UV spectrophotometer. Further, the pH of Cr(VI) solution was adjusted with 1 mol/L H2SO4 and 1 mol/L NaOH to investigate the effect of the initial solution pH on the photocatalytic efficiency (Wan et al., 2017).

All the electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature in a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell, consisting of Ag/AgCl (4 mol/L KCl) reference electrode, platinum wire counter electrode, and glass-carbon working electrode coated with a catalyst. The electrolyte consisted a mixture of 50 mmol/L Na2SO4 and 0.5 mmol/L PMS. Initially, a mixture consisting of 5 mg catalyst powder, 0.1 mL Nafion solution (5 wt.%, Sigma-Aldrich) and 1 mL absolute ethanol was dispersed by ultrasonic treatment to prepare a homogeneous catalyst ink. Then, an aliquot (5 μL) of the prepared catalyst ink were dropped on the surface of the working electrode, and dried in an indoor ambient air atmosphere. All the electrochemical data were collected on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (China). Amperometric current-time (I-t) curve was obtained at a bias of 0.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) under irradiation from a 300 W xenon lamp with a 420-nm cutoff (Chen et al., 2019).
The DFT calculations are shown in Appendix A. Supplementary data.

XRD analysis was used to investigate the crystal structure of the NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials. As shown in Fig. 1 , all the characteristic diffraction peaks of pure NaBiO3 nanosheets consisted of NaBiO3 (JCPDS card No. 73–0905). The characteristic peaks corresponding to the (104), (110), and (205) crystal planes (Selvarajan et al., 2018), indicating that the crystalline NaBiO3 nanosheets were successfully synthesized. For all the CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials, the strong irradiation peak indicated that the hybrid materials were well crystallized. After the addition of CQDs, a characteristic peak was observed at approximately 23.7°, which corresponds to the graphite (002) peak. The results of this study were consistent with the results from the previous studies (Di et al., 2015; Yu and Kwak, 2012; Huang et al., 2017b). As the amount of CQDs increased, the characteristic peak strength of NaBiO3 also increased with a slight shift in the peak position, indicating a significant change in the crystal structure of NaBiO3. The XRD results are in agreement with the morphological analysis results. Previously, it was shown that there is a strong interaction between CQDs and NaBiO3, and the crystal structure of NaBiO3 could be transformed by the interaction with CQDs. Thus, it can be stated that, CQDs were successfully loaded on the NaBiO3 nanosheets.

The morphology and microstructures of the pure NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 composites were investigated by SEM analysis. The structure of 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 was further investigated by TEM and HRTEM. The synthesized NaBiO3 nanosheets had a length of approximately 100–300 nm with a relatively smooth surface (Fig. 2 a). The large and smooth surface provides a suitable area for recombination with other materials. As shown in Fig. 2b and c, the large pieces of NaBiO3 nanosheets were interrupted to form small nanosheets and nanorods. Moreover, the surface was no longer smooth, after the addition of CQDs to the NaBiO3 nanosheets during the synthesis of hybrid materials by the hydrothermal method. These results were consistent with the results obtained from the XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 2d and e, the NaBiO3 nanosheets contain structures with ten dimensions of tens of nanometers. The surface of the composites with 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 consists of smaller sphere-like structures, with dimensions of less ten nanometers. Moreover, the sphere-like structures were evenly distributed on the surface of NaBiO3, providing strong evidence for the formation of the CQDs/NaBiO3 composites (Xie et al., 2018). The structures of the CQDs were further analyzed using HRTEM (Fig. 2f). The lattice spacing of the structures was close to 0.321 nm, corresponding to the graphite (002) crystal plane (Yu et al., 2011), which proved that the sphere-like structures were CQDs. Moreover, the graphite (002) crystal plane combined with the (310) crystal plane proved that the CQDs were successfully loaded on the NaBiO3 nanosheets (Selvarajan et al., 2018). Thus, it can be concluded that the CQDs/NaBiO3 composites were successfully synthesized.

XPS analysis was used to further confirm the surface composition and chemical state of the samples (Zhang et al., 2019). The wide survey scan XPS spectrum shows that sodium, Bi, oxygen, and carbon were present in the 6 wt.% CQD/NaBiO3 composites (Fig. 3 b), which was in agreement with the results from the mapping analysis. The high-resolution O 1 s, C 1 s, Bi 4f, and Na 1 s spectra were recorded for 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 and pure NaBiO3 samples (Fig. 3c-f). Three peaks, corresponding to lattice oxygen Bi-O, physical adsorbed oxygen and chemisorbed oxygen were observed in the O 1 s spectrum of 6 wt.% CQD/NaBiO3, and corresponding binding energies were 529.4, 530.6 and 531.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c) (Xia et al., 2010). Similarly, two peaks at the binding energies of approximately 529.4 and 530.6 eV were observed in the pure NaBiO3 sample, indicating that physically adsorbed oxygen was not detected after the addition of CQDs and suggesting that the crystal structure was destroyed (Ding et al., 2015). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of 6 wt.% CQD/NaBiO3 was divided into four peaks, which correspond to binding energies of 284.7, 285.8, 287.6, and 288.7 eV, respectively, representing CO, CO, COC, and CC bonds with sp2 orbital. Compared to the spectrum of pure NaBiO3, the C 1 s spectrum was split into two peaks with binding energies of 288.7 and 284.7 eV after the addition of CQDs, indicating that the two chemical bonds of CO and COC were formed (Ding et al., 2015). Generally, the two strong Bi 4f peaks centered at approximately 163.9 and 158.6 eV, correspond to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+, respectively (Di et al., 2015; Yoshitake and Tatsumi, 2003). On comparing the Bi 4f peak of CQDs/NaBiO3 composites and pure NaBiO3, a slight shift was observed in the Bi 4f peak of the CQDs/NaBiO3 composites. It indicated that the chemical environment of the Bi surface had changed, further confirming Bi-C interaction and the successful combination of CQDs and NaBiO3 (Ding et al., 2015). The CQDs/NaBiO3 complex was synthesized by the hydrothermal method in the NaOH aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 3f, the binding energy of Na ions was reduced after the addition of CQDs, because the Na ions in NaBiO3 were easily released in the NaOH solution, the crystal structure was destroyed and the sample structure tended to be unstable, eventually releasing O2-. In general, the presence of CQDs in the NaBiO3 lattice matrix resulted in the generation of oxygen vacancies to maintain electrostatic equilibrium (Ding et al., 2015). Therefore, XPS analysis further confirmed the successful synthesis of CQDs/NaBiO3 composites, which was consistent with the XRD and HRTEM results.

The adsorption and catalytic properties of the material were greatly affected by the surface area (Yoshitake and Tatsumi, 2003). Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were obtained to further characterize the NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 composites (Appendix A Fig. S2). The corresponding surface areas were calculated by BET was 15.756 and 22.804 cm3/g, respectively, which indicated that the surface area of the NaBiO3 nanosheets increased after the loading of CQDs. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), both the NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 composites followed the type-IV isotherm, with an H3 hysteresis loop, indicating that the material has a mesoporous structure and strongly interacts with nitrogen. Greater adsorption was observed at higher pressure, because of a slit hole formed by pushing a sheet of particles (Liu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2015). Compared with NaBiO3, the increased surface area and mesoporous structure of the composites may provide more active sites, thus enhancing its adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity (Yoshitake and Tatsumi, 2003).

The density of states (DOS) for NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 composites were calculated by applying density functional theory (DFT) to investigate the interfacial properties of the hybrid materials and the intrinsic mechanism involved in enhancement of the photocatalytic performance (Han et al., 2018, 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2013). The crystal structure model of NaBiO3 was constructed after optimization, as shown in Fig. 4 . Further, the distribution of the total DOS before and after doping with CQDs was compared (Fig. 5 a and b), latter increased in electronic state can be observed, from -16 to -4 eV. According to the partial DOS diagram (Appendix A Fig. S3) of carbon, which illuminated it made the mainly contribution. The valence band maximum (VBM) of NaBiO3 was mainly composed of O 2 s, O 2p, Bi 6 s and Na 3 s orbitals, and its conduction band minimum (CBM) was composed of Na 3 s and Bi 6p orbitals. The main components of the 6 wt.% CQD/NaBiO3 were Bi 6 s, Na 3 s, O 2 s, O 2p, C 2 s and C 2p orbitals, and the conduction bands were mainly consisted of hybrid Bi 6p, Na 3 s, and C 2p orbitals (Fig. 5). In addition, the largest peak density was observed for C 2p orbital at the bottom of the CB, indicating that a large number of electrons were concentrated in the CB, i.e., the electrons were stored in the 2p orbital (Yoshitake and Tatsumi, 2003).

The optical absorption characteristics of the NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials were investigated using UV–Vis. As shown in Appendix A Fig. S4, the absorption edge of the initial NaBiO3 nanosheets was approximately 600 nm, where that of the CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials extended to more than 750 nm, while there was an increased intensity after 750 nm, which is probably due to the lattice defects (such as oxygen vacancies) (Kou et al., 2008). The absorption of the samples resulted from the charge transfer transition from valence band (VB) to CB. As the content of CQDs increased, the light absorbing ability in the visible light region was enhanced. Compared with that of pure NaBiO3, the absorption of the composites exhibited a red shift, possibly due to the migration of electrons between NaBiO3 and CQDs. The increase in the visible light absorption of the composites can produce more electron-hole pairs for photocatalytic reactions.

The band gap energy (E g) is estimated by the following formula (Eq. (1)):

where α, h, v, and A are the absorption coefficient, discrete photon energy, and a constant, respectively. The index n is related to the type of optical transition in the gap region, where n = 1/2 and n = 2 represent direct bandgap and indirect bandgap semiconductor, respectively.

According to the previous studies, NaBiO3 belongs to the indirect band gap semiconductor, i.e., n = 2 (Kou et al., 2008; Di et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2012). The adsorption edge energies of NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 obtained were approximately 2.6 and 2.64 eV, respectively (Appendix A Fig. S4b). The composite material has a narrower band gap compared with that of pure NaBiO3, i.e., E g (eV) = 1240/g (nm), which induced a wider range of light responses and stronger interactions in the heterojunction nanostructures (Zhao et al., 2018).

The PL spectrum was obtained to verify the spread of the absorption range, the photogenerated charge separation, the rate of electron hole transfer, and the recombination rate, which can prove that the photocatalytic activity was enhanced. A weaker intensity illustrates lower photogenerated charge recombination (Sun et al., 2012). The PL spectra of NaBiO3 and CQDs/NaBiO3 are compared in Fig. 6 . Both the samples showed the stronger peak at approximately 470 nm. NaBiO3 exhibited a higher PL peak intensity, which indicated higher recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. On the other hand, there was a reduction in the peak intensity exhibited by the CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials, suggesting a decrease in the recombination rate of photogenerated charges (Li et al., 2018).

To further confirm the correctness of the above results, amperometric (I-t) curves were used to verify the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers (Appendix A. Supplementary data). It can be observed that the photocurrent response of the working electrode rises rapidly upon light irradiation, but declined immediately when the light source was turned off. Hybrid materials have a higher photocurrent compared with that of pure NaBiO3, which indicates that the hybrid materials have a higher separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes under visible light irradiation. Further, it also suggests that the loading of CQDs promoted the separation of electron-hole pairs (Zhang et al., 2017; Patnaik et al., 2018). It could be speculated that the photogenerated electrons were excited from the VB to the CB. Moreover, CQDs acted as electron sinks to accept the photogenerated electrons, thereby inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, probably because of the conjugated π structure of the CQDs (Di et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2017a).

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was evaluated in terms of the reduction of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 7 a, the adsorption rate of Cr(VI) in the hybrid composites and pure NaBiO3 samples reached equilibrium when the samples were initially subjected to dark conditions and then irradiated under visible light for 90 min. It is worth noting that Cr(VI) could not be reduced by pure NaBiO3, and only a small fluctuation was observed in the whole process, probably because of the experimental error. In contrast, a significant reduction in Cr(VI) was observed after the introducing CQDs to modify the NaBiO3 nanosheets. In the dark environment, the adsorption rate of the modified hybrid materials was improved. The adsorption rate increased with increasing content of CQDs, with the maximum adsorption rate reaching 40%. After the light was turned on, the trend observed was similar to the trend observed in the dark environment. It is interesting to note that 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 had the better Cr(VI) reduction efficiency. The reduction of Cr(VI) increased to approximately 80% after 10 min of visible light irradiation, and further increase to 97.7% after 90 min, indicating that Cr(VI) was almost completely reduced. It has to be note that there was a decrease in the reduction of Cr(VI) at the content of CQDs higher than 6%. It can be presumed that the increase in the content of CQDs changes the morphology of the original NaBiO3, leading to an increase in the active sites. However, continuous increase in CQDs reduces the material surface exposed to light as most of the active sites are occupied (Xie et al., 2018).

The different amounts of catalyst were added to a 50 mg/L Cr(VI) solution to determine the optimum reduction rate of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 7b, both the adsorption-desorption rate and the reduction efficiency increased after 90 min with the increase in the catalyst content. However, it is worth noting that the photocatalytic properties of the sample with different catalyst contents were similar under visible light irradiation, which was attributed to the four lines being almost parallel. Therefore, further experiments were performed with 1 g/L of the catalyst.

According to a previous study, the pH of the initial solution had a significant influence on the reduction of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 7c, there was an increase in the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) with a decrease in pH, with the optimal pH found to be equal to 3. However, as pH dropped below 2, the reduction efficiency drastically reduced, which was agreement with the results of the previous studies (Zhao et al., 2018; Yoshitake and Tatsumi, 2003; Xu et al., 2019). This phenomenon was mainly because of the following reason:

In the acidic solution, Cr(VI) exists in two ionic forms, Cr2O7 2− and HCrO4 −, whereas it exists as CrO4 2− in the alkaline solution (Liu et al., 2016). The surface of the catalyst is positively charged under the acidic conditions due to protonation, and it is easier to generate an electrostatic reaction to enhance adsorption (Xu et al., 2017).

In acidic solution:
(2) Cr2O7 2- +14H+ + 6e− → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
(3) HCrO4 − + 7H+ + 3e− → Cr3+ +4H2O

In alkaline solution:
(4) CrO4 2- + 4H2O + 3e− → Cr(OH)3 + 5OH−

Among them, Cr2O7 2−/Cr3+ (1.23 V vs. NHE (normal hydrogen electrode)), HCrO4 −/Cr3+ (1.35 V vs. NHE) and CrO4 2−/Cr3+ (-0.13 V vs. NHE) (Liu et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2015), the reduction potential in acidic solution was more positive than that of in the alkaline solution, which existed larger thermodynamic driving force, while in alkaline, according to reaction (4), Cr(OH)3 was formed on the surface of the catalyst, which lead to the active site was covered. Therefore, the acidic environment was easier for Cr(VI) reduction.

According to the Nernst equation, the following reactions occur in an acidic environment:

where φ is the equilibrium potential, φθ is the standard potential, [Cr2O7 2−], [H+], [Cr3+] and [H2O] is the concentration of Cr2O7 2−, protons, Cr3+, and H2O, respectively. In an acidic environment, the concentration of [H+] rose, the balance moved to the right (Huang et al., 2017b).

In natural waters, various kinds of ions are present. The influence of the ionic strength of the solution on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was evaluated in the presence of four anions in Cr(VI) solution (Fig. 7d). There was a substantial decrease in the reduction efficiency in the presence of CO3 2−, followed by HCO3 −, mainly because the adsorption was largely inhibited. Although SO4 2− and NO3 − ions had almost no effect on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency, these ions slightly improved the photocatalytic process.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to confirm the presence of reactive species in the composites when Cr(VI) was reduced under visible light irradiation. As shown in the Fig. 8 , the composite suspension in the spin-trap reagent (DMPO) aqueous solution was irradiated in visible light for 4 and 8 min. Both superoxide (•O2 −) and hydroxy (•OH) radicals were formed, and the signals of these radicals enhanced with the irradiation time. In the dark conditions, there was almost no signal of •O2 − and •OH radicals, indicating that visible light stimulates the formation of these two radicals, which may affect the photocatalytic performance.

It is well known that high specific surface area, high light absorption capacity, suitable VB and CB position, and efficient photo-generated carrier separation are all beneficial for enhancing the photocatalytic performance. Stronger light absorption capabilities were demonstrated, from the PL spectrum, the UV–Vis DRS spectrum, and the photocurrent response. The specific surface area of the pure NaBiO3 was 15.756 m2/g, whereas, that of 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 was 22.804 m2/g, i.e., 1.45 times that of the pure form. It could be presumed that there was an increase in the number of active sites in CQDs/NaBiO3 as its adsorption capacity was enhanced. However, the photocatalytic reduction efficiency after irradiation was much higher than 1.45 times. Thus, it can be inferred that the enhancement of the specific surface area was not the major influencing factor.

The above results were further confirmed by the active species capture experiments. As shown in Fig. 9 , silver nitrate (AgNO3) was used as a trapping agent for electrons− (e−) (Zhao et al., 2018). It was evident that the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) significantly decreased after the addition of AgNO3. After 90 min of visible light irradiation, the reduction efficiency was only close 40%, compared with that of observed in the absence of capture agents (i.e., approximately 98%). Thus, it can be inferred that the e− played a major role in the reduction of Cr(VI). After the addition of KI, used as a capture agent for holes (h+) (Zhao et al., 2018), there was a slight increase in the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) compared with that in the absence of capture agents. It could be assumed that h+ in the VB was captured by I−, thus accelerating the electron-hole pair and improving the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). The •O2 − radicals were trapped by benzoquinone (BQ) to determine their importance (Chang et al., 2010). It was clearly observed that after •O2 − radicals were trapped, there was a decrease in the reduction efficiency. Therefore, •O2 − play an important role in the photocatalytic reduction process. •O2 − was formed because the CB position of composites is -1.99 eV which is negative enough to reduce oxygen to •O2 −, i.e., E θ(O2/•O2 −) = -0.046 eV (E θ is oxidation/reduction potential) (Huang et al., 2017b). Isopropanol (TBA) was used as a scavenger for trapping •OH radicals (Xie et al., 2018). The results showed that the photocatalytic reduction performance was slightly suppressed after •OH radicals were trapped. •OH radical were generated because the VB position (2.47 eV) of NaBiO3 was more positive than E θ(•OH/OH−) (+1.99 eV vs NHE) (Ding et al., 2015), implying that OH− could be oxidized to •OH. The results from these experiments were consistent with the ESR results. The active species can be arranged in the order of importance as e− > •O2 − > h+ > •OH, indicating that e− and •O2 − were the major active species for the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI).

Based on the above experimental and characterization data, as shown in Fig. 10 , a possible pathway for the reduction of Cr(VI) under visible light was speculated. The results of UV–Vis DRS proved that the CQDs/NaBiO3 photocatalyst has excellent light absorption performance in the visible light region, probably because the up-conversion effect of CQDs improves the light absorption capacity (Huang et al., 2017a). Therefore, the CQDs/NaBiO3 photocatalyst may generate electron-hole pairs due to photon energy, leading to the excitation of electrons in the VB of NaBiO3 under visible light irradiation (Eq. (6)). Subsequently, electrons and holes migrate to the surface of the photocatalyst to participate in the redox reaction. At the same time, after the introduction of CQDs, some of the electrons of NaBiO3 could be transferred to CQDs, because the conjugated structure of CQDs could act as an electron acceptor. This, in turn, could inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Di et al., 2015). A similar hypothesis can be put forward based on the PL and I-t analysis results. During the photocatalytic process, the reduction potential of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) was 0.51 eV (vs. NHE) (Huang et al., 2017b), while the CB position of NaBiO3 was -0.19 eV (Selvarajan et al., 2018). Cr(VI) can be easily reduced to Cr(III) by photogenerated electrons, hence Cr(VI) in Cr2O7 2− was reduced to Cr(III) by the electrons in this process.
(6) NaBiO3 -CQDs + hv → e− + h+
(7) Cr2O7 2− + 14H+ + 6e− → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
(8) H2O + 2h+ → 1/2O2 + 2H+
For reaction (7), E θ (Cr2O7 2− / Cr3+) = 1.23 V vs. NHE.

In addition, the oxygen adsorbed on the hybrid materials was be reduced to superoxide radicals
(
O 2
−
)
by the electrons from the CB of hybrid materials, which accelerated the reduction of Cr(VI).

The stability and reusability of materials play an important role in the treatment of contaminated water. After three consecutive of recycling, the process was still very effective, and the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) was stable at approximately at 95% (Appendix A Fig. S6). Therefore, this study provides a valuable reference for the practical applications of composite materials in the future.

In this study, the novel CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid material was prepared for the first time using a simple hydrothermal synthesis. When CQDs are dispersed on the NaBiO3 nanosheets, they undergo strong interactions. After the loading of CQDs, the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) by NaBiO3 was significantly enhanced. The 6 wt.% CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid material exhibited the better photocatalytic performance. The measurements of photoelectrochemical, optical, and electronic properties showed that the composites had a higher photocurrent response, a wider UV–Vis response range, and a smaller fluorescence spectral response when compared to those of the pure material, indicating that the photogenerated electrons and holes were effectively separated, leading to the significant improvement in the photocatalytic activity. Our of all the active species, the e− played a significant role in the photocatalytic process. In addition, the CQDs/NaBiO3 hybrid materials exhibited excellent reusability and stability, implying a greater potential for environmentally friendly applications. This work would provide important insights into the development of other photocatalytic hybrid materials based on NaBiO3 and CQDs.
